title: SIEM Triage
challenge_data:
R001|CONDITION:event_type=auth_failure AND source=external|SEVERITY:high
R002|CONDITION:event_type=auth_success AND source=external AND details=root|SEVERITY:critical
R003|CONDITION:event_type=process_create AND details=powershell|SEVERITY:medium
R004|CONDITION:event_type=network_connect AND details=port=4444|SEVERITY:high|||2026-04-05T22:14:01|firewall|auth_failure|source=external user=admin
2026-04-05T22:14:03|syslog|auth_failure|source=external user=root
2026-04-05T22:14:07|syslog|auth_success|source=external user=root details=root
2026-04-05T22:15:12|sysmon|process_create|details=powershell -enc SQBFAFgA
2026-04-05T22:16:30|firewall|network_connect|details=dst=185.220.101.45 port=4444
2026-04-05T22:18:00|syslog|auth_failure|source=internal user=backup
challenge: What is the severity level of the highest-priority SIEM alert triggered by events in this log?
hints:
Go to https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/siem/guide/current/detections-ui-exceptions.html
        Read about SIEM correlation rules and alert severity levels.
        Correlation rules fire when ALL their conditions match a single event.
        Severity levels from lowest to highest: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL.

Match each event against the four active rules.
        R001 fires on auth_failure from external — check events 22:14:01 and 22:14:03.
        R002 fires on auth_success for root from external — check event 22:14:07.
        R003 fires on process_create with powershell — check event 22:15:12.
        R004 fires on network_connect to port 4444 — check event 22:16:30.
        Type 'tools' to run the SIEM correlator automatically.

Type 'tools' in the game. The SIEM correlator evaluates each event
        against all rules and lists every alert that fired with its severity.
        Find the alert with the highest severity level.

SPOILER — Event 22:14:07 (auth_success, source=external, user=root)
        matches R002 (CRITICAL). Root login from an external source is the
        most severe event in the log. Type: critical

steps.question: Look at the four correlation rules. Which rule (R001-R004) would fire on the event: '22:14:07 syslog auth_success source=external user=root'?
hints: Correlation rules fire when ALL their conditions match a single event. Check each rule against this event.
R001 requires auth_failure — this event is auth_success, so R001 does not fire.
R002 requires: event_type=auth_success AND source=external AND details=root. Check the event: auth_success? yes. external? yes. root? yes.
R002 fires on this event. All three conditions match: auth_success, source=external, user=root.

wrong_answer_hints.response: R001 fires on auth_failure. This event is auth_success — R001 does not match.
R003 fires on process_create with powershell. This is an auth_success event — R003 does not match.

steps.question: R002 fires at the CRITICAL severity level. The other events in the log also trigger rules R001, R003, and R004. What is the severity level of the highest-priority SIEM alert?
hints: SIEM alert severity levels from lowest to highest: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL
R001 fires at HIGH, R002 fires at CRITICAL, R003 fires at MEDIUM, R004 fires at HIGH
The highest severity among all fired alerts determines the queue priority
CRITICAL is the highest severity level — R002 firing at CRITICAL is the top-priority alert. Type: critical

wrong_answer_hints.response: HIGH is correct for R001 and R004, but there is a higher-severity alert in this queue — check R002.

steps.question: The CRITICAL alert fired on event '22:14:07 syslog auth_success source=external user=root'. What does this event describe in plain language?
hints: Break down the event fields: auth_success = successful login, source=external = from the internet, user=root = the root account
This is a successful login to the root account from an external (internet) source
In plain language: someone from outside the network successfully logged in as root
The CRITICAL alert describes a successful root login from an external IP — the NIGHTWIRE initial access.

tools: Evaluates each event against the active correlation rules and reports all alerts that fired with their severity levels.
learn:
SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) correlation rules are how
SOC analysts automate detection. A rule defines conditions — when all
conditions match an incoming event, an alert fires.

SIEM alert triage workflow:
  1. Sort alert queue by severity (CRITICAL first)
  2. Correlate related alerts into incidents (same source IP, same user)
  3. Identify true positives vs false positives
  4. Escalate confirmed incidents to incident tickets

Alert severity levels (typical):
  CRITICAL — requires immediate response (active exploitation, critical asset)
  HIGH     — investigate within 1 hour (lateral movement, C2 communication)
  MEDIUM   — investigate within 4 hours (reconnaissance, policy violation)
  LOW      — investigate within 24 hours (informational, single failed login)

False positive reduction:
  Correlation rules reduce false positives by requiring multiple conditions.
  Single-condition rules (any failed login) produce alert fatigue.
  Multi-condition rules (root login from external IP) are more precise.

This scenario uses a rule-based SIEM. Modern SIEMs also use:
  User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) — baseline + anomaly detection
  Machine learning models — unsupervised pattern detection
  Threat intel enrichment — automatic IOC matching

debrief.summary: The highest-priority alert is CRITICAL — rule R002 fired when root successfully authenticated from an external IP at 22:14:07. This is the NIGHTWIRE actor gaining initial foothold via compromised root credentials. The subsequent alerts (R003 PowerShell, R004 port 4444) confirm execution and C2 communication followed within minutes.
debrief.real_world: SIEM triage is the primary daily workflow for Tier 1 SOC analysts. In a real SOC, the CRITICAL alert would immediately escalate to a Tier 2 analyst and trigger an incident ticket. The correlated events (same 4-minute window, escalating severity) form a clear attack narrative.
debrief.next_step:
Practice with real SIEM tools:
TryHackMe: 'Investigating with ELK 101' room
https://tryhackme.com/room/investigatingwithelk101
debrief.cert_link:
CySA+ CS0-003 Domain 1 — Security Operations:
"Given a scenario, analyze output from common security tools used for SIEM monitoring, correlation, and alert triage."
debrief.exam_tip: On the exam, SIEM questions test two things: (1) which rule conditions match which event fields, and (2) which severity is highest. Remember severity order: CRITICAL > HIGH > MEDIUM > LOW. Also: correlation rules require ALL conditions to match — a rule with two conditions only fires when both are present in the same event.

scenario:
AEGIS TERMINAL v1.0 — VERIDIAN SYSTEMS SOC

INCOMING TICKET — SIEM PLATFORM
PRIORITY: CRITICAL
ANALYST: YOU

NIGHTWIRE INVESTIGATION — Project MERIDIAN

The SIEM is generating alerts. Your job is to triage the queue
and identify the most severe alert that fired during the
NIGHTWIRE activity window (22:14-22:18 UTC on 2026-04-05).

CORRELATION RULES ACTIVE:
  R001  auth_failure from external source           [HIGH]
  R002  auth_success for root from external source  [CRITICAL]
  R003  process_create with powershell              [MEDIUM]
  R004  network_connect to port 4444                [HIGH]

EVENT LOG (22:14-22:18 UTC):
  22:14:01  firewall  auth_failure     source=external user=admin
  22:14:03  syslog    auth_failure     source=external user=root
  22:14:07  syslog    auth_success     source=external user=root
  22:15:12  sysmon    process_create   powershell -enc SQBFAFgA
  22:16:30  firewall  network_connect  dst=185.220.101.45 port=4444
  22:18:00  syslog    auth_failure     source=internal user=backup

What is the SEVERITY of the highest-priority alert that fired?
